name: Cluster pod layout
description: Floor-layout strategy that groups rooms into 2-4 small clusters called pods, each holding 2-3 rooms that share a function and are linked together by a short hallway or archway. Every pod becomes a micro-zone of the building: a sleeping pod gathers bedrooms with their bathroom, a working pod fuses study and library, a service pod consolidates kitchen and pantry. Pods themselves are connected by brief circulation segments rather than one long corridor, so each cluster reads as a semi-private precinct. The pattern fits modern apartments where activity zones are deliberately separated, and monastic plans where cells ring a chapter house and refectory clusters sit apart from dormitory cells.
placement_rules: Define 2 to 4 pods on the footprint before placing any room; fewer collapses into a single open plan, more dissolves the clustering into a generic floor.
Each pod must contain 2 or 3 rooms that share a function (sleep, work, service); mixing unrelated rooms inside one pod breaks the micro-zone logic.
Rooms within the same pod open onto a shared antechamber or directly onto each other, so internal pod traffic never crosses another pod.
Link adjacent pods with a short corridor or archway no longer than 3 blocks; longer connectors turn the plan back into a hallway-spine layout.
Keep at least one block of wall thickness or a door threshold between pods so each cluster reads as an enclosed precinct rather than a continuous space.
Assign one pod-type per cluster (sleeping pod, working pod, service pod) and label it consistently in the floor plan metadata so downstream agents place furniture coherently.
Locate service pods (kitchen, pantry, bathroom) adjacent to external walls for plumbing/venting; sleeping pods should sit furthest from the entrance to honour the intimacy gradient.
